summary: Modal's product blog is technically accurate—features exist and are testable—but it's pure vendor marketing masquerading as news with zero independent verification. You're reading an advertisement, not journalism.
career_angle: Useful as a feature roadmap if you already use Modal, but meaningless for evaluating Modal against competitors; requires independent benchmarking and third-party reviews to assess actual value.

dimensions.name: Source Authority
dimensions.rationale: Modal speaks authoritatively on their own product features, but authority ≠ honesty. Company blogs are inherently promotional—they're announcing only what makes them look good.

dimensions.name: Evidence Quality
dimensions.rationale: Product announcements describe features without independent validation. No third-party benchmarks, no comparative analysis against competitors, no customer outcome data—just 'we shipped X.'

dimensions.name: Reproducibility
dimensions.rationale: Feature existence is immediately verifiable (sign up and test), making false claims about shipping features nearly impossible. This is actually the least BS part of vendor announcements.

dimensions.name: Conflict of Interest
dimensions.rationale: Extreme conflict of interest. This is Modal announcing their own features in their own channel. Every incentive to hype, zero incentive to mention limitations, competitive gaps, or price-to-value trade-offs.

dimensions.name: Consensus
dimensions.rationale: No expert consensus applies to a single vendor's product announcement. General serverless/GPU compute trends are established, but not specifically whether Modal's execution is competitive.

dimensions.name: Specificity
dimensions.rationale: Feature names are specific ('GPU memory snapshots,' 'notebooks,' 'service tokens'), but product announcements rarely specify edge cases, performance trade-offs, or limitations. Vague on what matters for adoption.

dimensions.name: Track Record
dimensions.rationale: Modal is funded, shipping, and doesn't have a public history of false claims. Vendor blogs rarely claim features they didn't ship. The BS isn't about lying—it's about selective framing.
